draft.introduction: Justinian’s Institutes (533 CE) is the official textbook of Roman law, condensing centuries of jurisprudence into an accessible manual. Its opening definition of justice as “the constant and perpetual will to render to each his due” shaped legal thought for fifteen centuries.

translation.text: Justice is the constant and perpetual will to render to each his due. Jurisprudence is the knowledge of things divine and human, the science of the just and the unjust. The precepts of the law are these: to live honorably, to harm no one, and to render to each his due.
translation.original_text: Iustitia est constans et perpetua voluntas ius suum cuique tribuendi

translator_notes.note: The threefold precept—honeste vivere, alterum non laedere, suum cuique tribuere (live honestly, harm no one, give each his due)—derives from Ulpian and became the most quoted summary of natural law in Western jurisprudence. Aquinas, Grotius, and the Enlightenment natural lawyers all build on this foundation.
